data.meaning_mnemonic: You have two lines with legs below them. And what is that bit above the legs? Well, it's where you came from — your <radical>origin</radical>. Yep, between two legs is where you originated, no matter how you look at it.

Have you ever used one of those triangular rulers? This looks just like one of those, because this is the <radical>measurement</radical> radical. Just think of all the angles you could measure with this thing!

This radical technically means person (like 人), but we're going to shift the meaning a little bit. It's kind of like the person radical, but it's taller and skinnier, standing above the others and raising its fist to lead them. That's why this is a <radical>leader</radical>, not a regular person.

Sure, this <radical>forehead</radical> is a bit square, but that's because it's the top part of the head (冂) radical. This is just the forehead part of that! You can even imagine this as the forehead of Frankenstein's monster, since his forehead was pretty square too.

Also, don't confuse this for a roof. You know this is a forehead and not a roof because this doesn't have a cute little chimney sticking out of the top. Foreheads definitely don't have chimneys.
See the mouth and long, fang-like teeth of this <radical>alligator</radical>, which is facing to the right? You wouldn't want to get your hand stuck in there, that's for sure.

This radical looks like a person with a long dress running behind them. That's because they're all dressed up to go to a <radical>ceremony</radical>. You usually have to wear a long dress (or something formal) if you're going to a big ceremony, right? So when you see this radical, think of all the different ceremonies you might have to go to in your life and what you'll need to wear.

This radical looks like some sort of <radical>spirit</radical>. It faces to the left with a big hood over its head, and creepy arms slanting downward at the sides. Can you picture it?

If not, just think of this as the katakana character for "ne." Ne for necromancer, bringing back spirits from the grave! OooooOOoooOoo!

There's a cross branching off of this stool. Since it's branching out, we'll just call this the <radical>branch</radical> radical. I guess the tree this stool was made of is coming back to life in the form of a branch.

This is the radical for <radical>energy</radical>. If you imagine a train under this radical, then the lines would be steam bursting out of it, flowing back from the front of the train (on the left) as it goes chugging along. Steam was the best way to generate energy back in the day, you know? That's why this is the radical for energy.

Use your imagination to draw a circle outside this shape. The grids from the radical start to look like longitude and latitude lines on a globe, don't they? That's why this is the <radical>world</radical>.

This is a few plates drying on a rack. They're stacked together in a line. Imagine reaching out and grabbing one of those plates for yourself. And you only need one, so let's call this radical <radical>plate</radical>.
See how tall the stalks are with little bushy things at the top? It kind of looks like <radical>bamboo</radical>, if you use your imagination.

In some poop you find something small. You lean in, carefully pull it out, and realize it's a <radical>thread</radical>. What's that doing there? Well, it came from a cat, and cats love to eat thread when you aren't looking. And floss and hoodie strings and mouse tails… But this time, just thread.

This is the <radical>ear</radical> radical, since it looks like an ear. As much as a radical can, anyway. Those two middle lines are the ridges that guide the sound into your ear, and the lines that stick out to the right are where the ear attaches to your head.

If you think about it, ears are just eyes that see sound. Whoa, dude… That explains the eye (目) and ear (耳) radical resemblance. Just remember this one is "ear" because ears stick out, just like the lines sticking out of the side of this radical.

This looks like the middle radical on top of a sideways leaf. What can you often find sitting on the middle of a leaf? An <radical>insect</radical>. Insects looove sitting right in the middle of leaves. That's why this is the insect radical.

See the little things sticking out of the ground? Those little things are pretty little <radical>flowers</radical>.

You could also see this as a little flower crown, especially since this radical almost always appears at the top of a kanji.

This eye has legs, since you have to let your eyes run over something in order to <radical>see</radical> it. That's why those eyes need legs — to see. They're wandering eyes, they want to see the world!!

A pile of dirt fell on you and now your legs and (scuba) fins are sticking out from under it, turning <radical>red</radical>. You know, because you've been stuck in the sun so long. Everything's all sunburned and red. Can scuba fins get a sunburn? Yeah, when it's red hot outside. That's why this means "red."

Doesn't this look like a top-down view of the frame of a <radical>car</radical>? There are the axles at the top and bottom, and the body in the middle between them. That's why this is the car radical.

This radical looks like a <radical>shellfish</radical>. What looks like the eye radical is its long spiral shell. The bottom part that looks like fins is its little legs poking out, helping the shellfish move around.

This is the <radical>foot</radical> radical. See the person doing a flying dragon kick to the right? What do they hit the target with? Obviously their foot. Imagine that foot coming straight at your face. At that point, your only hope is that it's clean. Nobody wants a foot to the face, especially not a filthy foot.

This looks like a regular arrow, but it's not. Let's call it the "not arrow." It can not move forward, because it's been stopped by some kind of line above it. Every arrow wants to fly, but this one is not going anywhere. Look at it, being all negated like that. Well, that's just what this radical does. It nots things.

The <radical>samurai</radical> radical looks a lot like the dirt radical. The difference is this one has a longer top horizontal line, which is for those broad warrior shoulders. The bottom line is also shorter, since it's the little cushion the samurai is sitting on. Samurai didn't just fight, you know. They sat on cushions and wrote poems and stuff too. This is a very sensitive samurai.

This is the radical for <radical>rice</radical>. Specifically raw rice, like the grain that grows out of the ground when rice is raw and fresh. When rice is particularly ripe, it'll kind of look like this at the top of the stalks. See the long leaves and the little rice tufts at the top? That's why this radical is "rice."

The leader (ｲ) radical has a little something extra on top of him — he's got a casual cap on because it's his day off. And what does he like to do on his day off? He likes to <radical>loiter</radical>. Look at him just loitering around. What a loiterer.

You'll have to complete the line on the right side in your mind, but see the rectangle <radical>bed</radical> and the pillows? This is totally a bed. That missing line is where you pulled back the covers when you got up this morning.
Obviously, this looks just like the good ol' 3.14 something-something all people are supposed to learn about. It's the math symbol that has to do with circles... <radical>pi</radical>!
See the <radical>cliff</radical>? It's facing to the left. What do you think this cliff is jutting out over? Maybe an ocean, or a valley? Cliffs love to jut out over things like that.

The king is hovering a drop of water over his head. He's acquired the ability to manipulate water at will, making him the <radical>master</radical> of all water! If you think about how all life depends on water, that really makes him the master of everything…

You have a roof on top of what looks like a volcano. What's the roof covering? It's definitely covering the hole at the top. I guess someone thought if they built a roof on the hole, none of the lava could come out. But isn't there a hole in the chimney? Guess their theory had some holes in it… Oh no, now lava is coming through the hole. RUN!!! RUN!!!!!!!!
This radical looks like an <radical>umbrella</radical>, specifically just the handle part. A good old curvy umbrella handle, so you can hang that umbrella on your arm when it stops raining.

Remember the guy from the big radical? Here he's bending his arms at the elbow to fasten some kind of belt, right across the <radical>center</radical> of his body. It's one of those belts that just goes across your waist, which is actually perfect for such a big guy, since he really needs to secure his center (of gravity).

A rice paddy needs power, and that power comes from a <radical>man</radical>. Picture a rice paddy full of thick, wet mud. It's no easy field to work in, but this powerful man will bend over to rake and hoe tirelessly to make sure it thrives. What a man!
The <radical>stone</radical> radical and <kanji>stone</kanji> kanji are the same thing!
data.meaning_hint: Know your radicals and therefore know your kanji. It's just that simple.
data.reading_mnemonic: You throw a <kanji>stone</kanji> through a window... and, now you can see something very <reading>sexy</reading> (せき).
data.reading_hint: Whoops, maybe you shouldn't throw stones through windows or you'll see people's sexy times.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>origin</radical> radical and the <kanji>origin</kanji> kanji are the same!
data.meaning_hint: Know your radicals!
data.reading_mnemonic:
The <kanji>origin</kanji> of everything can be found in <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). Genji is the name of the main character in the world's first ever novel, "The Tale of Genji." 

Picture him as a rich, pretty boy who cares a ton about his looks and gets along really well with all the ladies. He's also really great at poetry, which helps the lady situation.
data.reading_hint: Genji's origin story is pretty important when it comes to mnemonics and Japanese literature. He's going to keep popping up on your kanji journey, so make sure you remember him!

data.meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>world</radical> radical and the <kanji>world</kanji> kanji are the same, but there is another really important meaning you need to know: <kanji>generation</kanji>. 

Think of it this way: the world is just made up of many different generations of people, plants, and animals all getting along together.
data.meaning_hint: It's all these generational differences that make the world go 'round!
data.reading_mnemonic: It's your job to protect the <kanji>world</kanji> and every <kanji>generation</kanji> with your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). It was given to you to uphold correctness, and you'll pass it on to the next generation when it's time. Then they'll watch out for the world when you're gone.
data.reading_hint: Hold the saber up high and pledge to protect the world for all of the generations to come! Then swish it around and make buzzing sounds with your mouth.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
The radical <radical>master</radical> and the kanji <kanji>master</kanji> have the same meanings!

This kanji also means <kanji>main</kanji> because the master is the main person of a dojo or household or business.
data.reading_mnemonic: Do you remember back to the radical mnemonic, where the <kanji>master</kanji> is floating a water drop above his head? Imagine back to there. Once you do, the most amazing thing happens. The master sends the drop of water into a <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ) and it goes careening through an intricate series of them.
data.reading_hint: Picture this master of water controlling the droplet as it flies around inside these chutes, like he's solving a difficult puzzle with it. Astounding!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>alligators</radical>... she's no ordinary leader, that's for sure. She's not a politician, she's not a queen... She's one of the <kanji>Other</kanji> Ones, a group of strange, eccentric people who become leaders of animals.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine different groups of leaders — they're all dressed nice and normal. Then, off to the side are the leaders of bears, cats, alligators, and narwhals... They're the other ones, no matter how you look at it. They are in their own group, and they are considered "others" because society won't accept them :(
data.reading_mnemonic:
To remember the reading た, we use the word "taco":

Then there's the <kanji>other</kanji>s of the others. These are the <reading>ta</reading>co (た) worshipers. They're really strange.
data.reading_hint: Just imagine the "others" shaking their heads at these taco worshipers. Seriously, what the heck?

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>forehead</radical> of this <radical>beggar</radical> is on the <radical>ground</radical>. Except it's not exactly the ground, it's a giant <kanji>copy</kanji> machine, and he's making copies of his forehead!
data.meaning_hint: Picture how ridiculous this beggar looks, taking copies of his forehead with the ground copy machine.
data.reading_mnemonic: This ground <kanji>copy</kanji> machine can only be the work of one person: a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ)!
data.reading_hint: This particular shaman's specialty is being able to turn any surface into a copy machine at a swirl of his little shaman wand. Picture him running around in circles, doing a little dance and chant, and then the ground suddenly turning into one giant copy machine.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If someone tries to dig up <radical>dirt</radical> on your <radical>private</radical> life, tell them no thanks, that stuff is in the <kanji>past</kanji>. Some things should stay in the past. Not everyone needs to know everything about your life and your past.
data.meaning_hint: Keep your private dirt in the past. It's better for everyone.
data.reading_mnemonic: To remember the reading きょ, we'll use "Little Kyoto." This will differentiate きょ from きょう, which is regular-sized Kyoto. Get it? You decide to bury all this private dirt from your <kanji>past</kanji> in Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). That stuff is from when you were just a little <reading>子</reading> (こ) and you don't like to think about it.
data.reading_hint: Imagine physically, literally burying your past 子 self. If you hide it in Little Kyoto, no one will ever be able to dig it up.

data.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> in the <radical>ground</radical> (in our case, it's a hatch), and inside is some kind of <radical>prison</radical> you can't quite get to. If you've ever seen Lost, this will sound familiar... on the hatch, there's actually a series of <kanji>number</kanji>s that you don't understand.
data.meaning_hint: What are these numbers? What do they mean? Think over it for a while, get frustrated, and try to figure it out. You can't, though, so you probably won't ever get inside.
data.reading_mnemonic:
"You want to know the <kanji>number</kanji> to the hatch?" yells an old voice from inside. You say "yes," and the voice gives you the numbers and you're in! You go down into the prison, where there is one prisoner: Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち.
data.reading_hint: Grandpa ごういち keeps saying numbers as you free him from the prison. Just one after another. Numbers numbers numbers. Hopefully old man ごういち isn't crazy.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The radical for <radical>center</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>center</kanji> are exactly the same.
data.meaning_hint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji as well! How swell.
data.reading_mnemonic: Remember that guy from the 央 radical fastening his belt across the <kanji>center</kanji> of his body? This guy always secures his belt at the center — not slightly to the left, not slightly to the right, but dead center. His precision is so mind-blowing that everyone watching can't help but say, "<reading>Ohh</reading>!"(おう).
data.reading_hint: Next time you put a belt on, make sure it's in the exact center of your body. Heck, try it now. And don't forget to say "Ohh!" as you do it. It'll keep the reading for this kanji front and center in your mind.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If you want to <radical>dry</radical> your <radical>horns</radical> you need to lay them <kanji>flat</kanji>. If they aren't flat they'll always be a little damp somewhere and you'll never have a moment's <kanji>peace</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine carefully taking off your horns and laying them flat on a paper towel. You have to smooth them out so they're as flat as possible.
data.reading_mnemonic: Being <kanji>flat</kanji> isn't enough, so you cover the whole thing in <reading>hay</reading> (へい). Hay absorbs water, so you add a flat layer of it.
data.reading_hint: Imagine carefully laying a flat layer of hay on top of your flat horns. You feel really at peace and... you're tired now. You decided to lie on top of the hay and take a nap while you wait for your horns to dry.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical> to the <radical>street</radical> to <kanji>hit</kanji> some people! Street fingers are for curling into a fist. And that street fist wants to do some hitting!
data.meaning_hint: Imagine: it's just you, your fingers, and the wild street. It's hitting time!
data.reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>hit</kanji> one chump after another, suddenly your <reading>da</reading>d (だ) comes marching out of your house. Uh oh. Dad caught you hitting, which, honestly, isn't a very nice thing to do, so he has every right to stop you.
data.reading_hint: Now imagine your dad saying, "Don't do a hit!" over and over at you. "I'm sorry dad! But the streets were calling me!"

data.meaning_mnemonic: Add a <radical>drop</radical> of something magical to <radical>water</radical> and suddenly it turns into big block of <kanji>ice</kanji>, just like that.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself adding that drop. What is it exactly? Something pretty potent, that's for sure. Now go ahead and touch the block of ice... wow, it's really cold — and only from one drop, too. Feel the burn on your finger from where you touched the <kanji>ice</kanji>.
data.reading_mnemonic: When you think of <kanji>ice</kanji>, I want you to think of someone frozen with ice. Do you know anyone named <reading>Corey</reading> (こおり)? Anyone famous named Corey, perhaps Corey Feldman? Imagine this Corey person frozen solid with ice.
data.reading_hint: How did this happen? Was it your fault? Imagine the situation that would get Corey frozen like this.

data.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>cross</radical> inside your <radical>mouth</radical>, so the things you say are very pious. Everything you say, you <kanji>say humbly</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself with a cross inside your mouth, saying all kinds of humble things. "Oh no, I'm not that good." "Oh no, my mustache doesn't look that great."
data.reading_mnemonic: As you're saying all these humble things, you're also <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) your neighbor's lawn. Your neighbors love you so much, because you're so humble, and you mow their lawn for them.
data.reading_hint: Imagine mowing your neighbor's lawn while you say humbly how bad you are at mowing lawns. What a good neighbor you are.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <kanji>plate</kanji> kanji and the <radical>plate</radical> radical are exactly the same, like two matching pieces of china.
data.meaning_hint: If you know your radicals, you know your kanji too.
data.reading_mnemonic: The person washing all the <kanji>dish</kanji>es was named <reading>Sarah</reading> (さら). She was named that because of her large head, which was shaped like Sarah Jessica Parker’s head (very big).
data.reading_hint: Imagine someone with a huge head, washing dishes. When I say huge, I mean huge. She turns towards you with her big head and says “Hi, I’m Sarah” and you see her name tag, which also says Sarah. She is Sarah, the dish washer. The plates she holds up next to her head pale in comparison, even though they're the biggest pieces.

data.meaning_mnemonic: What do you get if you lend a <radical>spirit</radical> your <radical>umbrella</radical>? <kanji>Thanks</kanji>, of course! Especially if that spirit is Totoro, the spirit of the forest.
data.meaning_hint: It's raining, so you lend Totoro your umbrella. As a thanks, he gives you a present wrapped in bamboo leaves.
data.reading_mnemonic: So what did you receive as a <kanji>thanks</kanji>? You open it up, and there's a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun inside!
data.reading_hint: Thanks for the new ray gun, Totoro!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> is next to the <radical>tree</radical>. What is he doing? He's leaning against it, <kanji>rest</kanji>ing.
data.meaning_hint: The best thing to do with a tree is rest under it. Picture the leader resting under the tree with his eyes half closed.
data.reading_mnemonic: As he <kanji>rest</kanji>s, he suddenly gets hungry and pulls a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) off the tree and bites into it. A good rest isn't complete without some crunchy tree cucumbers.
data.reading_hint: Don't email me saying cucumbers don't grow on trees. Cucumber trees exist. Look them up and then rest easy knowing I'd never lie to you.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If you go down a <radical>slide</radical> and get <radical>dirt</radical> on your <radical>legs</radical>, it must have been the fault of the <kanji>previous</kanji> person, aka the person who went down the slide <kanji>ahead</kanji> of you. They should've cleaned off their dirty legs <kanji>before</kanji> they went down the slide, but they didn't, and now you've got dirt all over your legs too.
data.meaning_hint: Try to imagine the previous person — they must have been absolutely filthy to leave all this dirt behind. Why'd they have to go down the slide ahead of you?! If only you could've gotten here before them…
data.reading_mnemonic: Then you realize: the <kanji>previous</kanji> person on the slide must've been a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Centaurs track dirt everywhere 'cause they're always galloping around in the wilderness.
data.reading_hint: If you look really far off into the distance ahead of you, you might still be able to spot this dirty, slide-ruining centaur. Hurry up and go after him before he gets on another slide!

data.meaning_mnemonic: What is the <radical>evening</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>? Imagine a mouth that's up in the night sky. It's just there, talking, and only comes out at night. It only appears in one area of the world. (What area would you choose? Imagine yourself going to that place to see the "evening mouth.") And it appears only one month out of the year (more on this in a second). The "evening mouth" is the most famous thing in the _____ area (this is the area you choose). He's so <kanji>famous</kanji> that everybody knows his <kanji>name</kanji> ("Evening Mouth").
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself going to the area you chose, and watching "Evening Mouth" speak. There are thousands of others there, wanting to see the giant floating mouth in the night sky. "Evening Mouth" then says "Everyone, what's my <kanji>name</kanji>?!" and everyone shouts back "Evening Mouth!" in unison. "Evening Mouth" has certainly gotten really <kanji>famous</kanji>.
data.reading_mnemonic: So, when does “Evening Mouth” appear? Turns out, “Evening Mouth” only comes out to talk in <reading>May</reading> (めい). You know this because it’s just starting to be spring, and when you look around (you’re watching “Evening Mouth” again) you see some spring flowers come up.
data.reading_hint: You know what they say. April showers bring May Flowers. What do <reading>May</reading> Flowers bring? They bring “Evening Mouth,” baby.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>gun</radical> and a <radical>cow</radical>, which must mean it's that time of the <kanji>year</kanji>! Every year on New Year's Eve, you get asked to shoot a cow, as per tradition. You really don't want to though, so you deliberately miss every time.
data.meaning_hint: Last year was a close call and you ended up shooting the cow's horn, which is why the cow radical looks a little strange here. Can you see how the cow radical fits underneath the gun?
data.reading_mnemonic: It seems everyone has caught on to the fact that you deliberately miss every <kanji>year</kanji>, so this year you make a <reading>nen</reading>doroid (ねん) decoy in the shape of a cow.
data.reading_hint: Nendoroids are Japanese plastic figurines. (Look them up if you need to! And yes, there are cow nendoroids.) It probably won’t be easy to make a cow-sized nendoroid this year, but better than having to shoot a real one.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> is above the <radical>cross</radical>, it's time for some people to go church — which is always way too <kanji>early</kanji> on a Sunday, am I right? And if you want to get to church early, you need to be <kanji>fast</kanji>, otherwise you'll be too late.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine the sun coming up slowly, over the horizon. It then lines up with the cross, and at that moment, your alarm goes off, waking you up. You check the alarm clock, and instead of displaying the time, it just says EARLY and FAST on it, flashing in big red letters and alternating.
data.reading_mnemonic:
To remember the そう reading we use the word "soul."

When you're forced to get up <kanji>fast</kanji> and <kanji>early</kanji> you can feel it in your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). You're so tired and it's so early, your soul cries out, "Please don't make me go this fast this early. I'm gonna leave your body if you keep this up!"
data.reading_hint: But you get your soul up early and head to the church fast anyway, because you're doing all of this for your soul in the first place!

data.meaning_mnemonic: These days <radical>energy</radical> is the greatest <radical>treasure</radical>. Not gold, money, or anything like that. Imagine finding some in a literal treasure chest. As soon as you open it, out bursts the <kanji>energy</kanji> in a kind of <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball.
data.meaning_hint: Touch the energy, catch the spirit ball. Is it solid or gaseous? Does it tingle or burn? Try to imagine it out. Then, yes, go ahead and sell that valuable energy treasure to a power company or something.
data.reading_mnemonic: As you reach inside the <kanji>energy</kanji> <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball, you feel something hard inside. It's a large <reading>key</reading> (き) that gives you access to another treasure.
data.reading_hint: You take the key and open another treasure that's nearby. Open it up and see what's inside. Imagine something spectacular. Thanks, energy key.

data.meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>leaf</radical> growing out of the <radical>sun</radical>! You fly out into space to check it out and when you get closer you see that it isn't just one leaf, it's a <kanji>hundred</kanji>! That makes sense, you wouldn't be able to see just one leaf on the sun from where you were on earth.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine flying toward the sun to see a hundred leaves growing together in the shape of one big leaf. You want to go closer!
data.reading_mnemonic: You fly closer, counting all one <kanji>hundred</kanji> leaves when you hear a strange sound. You take off your space helmet and hear, "GUH <reading>HYUCK</reading> (ひゃく)!" You know that laugh... it's Goofy! On top of the hundredth leaf is Goofy laughing and waving around. He must have built this leaf tower to get your attention.
data.reading_hint: Imagine hearing Goofy guh-hyuck over and over after you save him from being stranded on the sun. How would you feel being stuck in a cramped ship with laughing Goofy and his hundred leaves? Oh, you thought he would leave those behind? Nope, they saved him so you have to take them back too. Happy travels!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>bamboo</radical> radical and the <kanji>bamboo</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
data.meaning_hint: Just make sure you know your radicals!
data.reading_mnemonic: Today is free <kanji>bamboo</kanji> day, so just <reading>take</reading> (たけ) all the bamboo you want.
data.reading_hint: Really take as much bamboo as you could possibly hold. Take some bamboo, then take some more, and take more… It’s free after all.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
data.meaning_hint: This is why it's good to make sure you know your radicals.
data.reading_mnemonic: You have some <kanji>thread</kanji>... what's the first thing you do with it? You take it and put it in your mouth, and start sucking it down like spaghetti. You have decided to <reading>eat</reading> (いと) the thread.
data.reading_hint: Think about how it tastes. Think about how it feels in your throat. Let it get caught on your teeth and around your tongue. It's a weird feeling having thread in your mouth, and you want to use that weird feeling to remember you're going to <reading>eat</reading> it.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>ear</radical> radical and <kanji>ear</kanji> kanji are the same in looks as well as meaning.
data.meaning_hint: Study those radicals hard to make sure you know your kanji as well!
data.reading_mnemonic: Put your hand to your <kanji>ear</kanji> and sing... "<reading>Mimi</reading>mimimimiiiii!" (みみ) and listen to your tone with your ear.
data.reading_hint: This is sort of a stereotypical singer sort of image. Make sure you concentrate a lot when you're listening to your mimimimiiii tone. In fact, why don't you do it out loud right now - make a little fool of yourself. It's fun, and it'll help you to remember!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>insect</radical> radical and <kanji>insect</kanji> are the same in looks as well as and meaning.
data.meaning_hint: It's times like these when you gotta hope you're studying your radicals!
data.reading_mnemonic: It's an <kanji>insect</kanji>! Get it! You squish it with your foot - what sound does it make? A <reading>mush</reading> (むし) sound.
data.reading_hint: Imagine little words that say "mush" flying out from under your foot as you mush the poor thing. What does a "mush" sound sound like? You'll have to come up with something unique to yourself, and focus on it to remember this reading.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Someone's taking <radical>tree</radical> <radical>measurements</radical>… Why are they taking measurements of trees? Because they're planning to cut them down to make their <kanji>village</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: There is a whole group of people out there who are measuring these trees. Imagine them asking each other "do you think this will be a good tree for our village center?" and other people nodding in agreement or disagreement as they measure.
data.reading_mnemonic: They take all the wood back to where they're building their <kanji>village</kanji>, and once they construct the village center they paint a <reading>mura</reading>l (むら) on the side of it. The mural is of the forest where they got the trees from, to honor the village's origins.
data.reading_hint: Look at the mural on the side of this newly constructed village center. So many trees! Visualize the whole process of the tree becoming a building, as you reflect on the message of the mural.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The radical for <radical>man</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>man</kanji> are exactly the same.
data.meaning_hint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji too! Man, kanji are easy sometimes.
data.reading_mnemonic: When you think of a <kanji>man</kanji>, think of <reading>dawn</reading> (だん), because that man has to get up at dawn to power his rice paddy every day.
data.reading_hint: Picture the man watching as the first light of dawn tints the sky pink and orange. Time to get to work, man!

data.meaning_mnemonic: When you see both a <radical>rice paddy</radical> and a <radical>street</radical> in the same place, you have the makings of a whole <kanji>town</kanji>!
data.meaning_hint: Think about it, a single farm wouldn't need a whole street. Just paths would do. There is a street next to this rice paddy, so there must be other people, which makes this a <kanji>town</kanji>.
data.reading_mnemonic: You go to visit this nice little <kanji>town</kanji>. But then, there she is... Every time you walk down the street, the street-woman Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) arrives to ruin your day.
data.reading_hint: Remember imagining Mrs. Chou in great detail in level 2? Time for some of that work to come to fruition. Imagine Mrs. Chou walking down the street of the town towards you, ready to make your day bad. She breaks things as she walks towards you, then knocks you down for walking around in her town. She says, "Wherever there's a street, you'll find Mrs. Chou." Then spits on your back as she walks away.

data.meaning_mnemonic: All these <radical>flowers</radical> were planted by your <radical>leader</radical> with nothing but a <radical>spoon</radical>. Now the whole place is absolutely full of beautiful <kanji>flower</kanji>s. Flowers everywhere.
data.meaning_hint: Your leader is a nice guy who wants there to be flowers for future generations to enjoy. That's why he's painstakingly planting all these flowers with his spoon. Imagine watching him dig hole after hole with his little spoon just to plant some flowers for everyone.
data.reading_mnemonic: Secretly, the leader is actually planting all these <kanji>flower</kanji>s for <reading>Hannah</reading> (はな), a girl he has a huge crush on. Everyone thinks the leader's a nice, selfless guy, but really he's just trying to impress Hannah.
data.reading_hint: You and everyone else are free to enjoy the flowers too, but… Just make sure to get out of the way when Hannah shows up. These flowers are really for her.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!
data.meaning_hint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. If you do, you'll know a lot of kanji as well, almost magically!
data.reading_mnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is <reading>me</reading> (み)!"
data.reading_hint: The struggling to see bit is pretty important — make sure that you slowly see a little better and better as the person comes closer, but it's difficult the whole time (struggles are good for memory). Then, it starts to become more and more clear, and you start saying "that can't be <reading>me</reading>. No way it's me..." When the person comes up to you (the person, being you) you learn about parallel universes and other fabulous things. Still, the whole time you can't stop thinking about how "this person looks just like me... no, this person is me!"

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>shellfish</radical> radical and the <kanji>shellfish</kanji> kanji are the same! It can be just the <kanji>shell</kanji> part too.
data.meaning_hint: Shellfish are shellfish, you know?
data.reading_mnemonic: To get some <kanji>shellfish</kanji>, all you have to do is hop in your <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) and get some. Shellfish live in the water.
data.reading_hint: Imagine rowing your kayak out into the ocean to collect shellfish. Imagine the color and shape of the kayak. Feel the spray of the salt water and the taste of some fresh shellfish, right out of the ocean.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The radical for <radical>red</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>red</kanji> are the same thing!
data.meaning_hint: What a lucky ducky — as long as you know your radicals, you'll know this kanji's meaning as well!
data.reading_mnemonic: This kanji means <kanji>red</kanji>, <reading>AKA</reading>, also known as, あか.
data.reading_hint: Think of あか as being some sort of alias for "red." That's why it's pronounced あか in Japanese, AKA "red."

data.meaning_mnemonic:
The radical for <radical>foot</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>foot</kanji> are exactly the same.

That being said, there's also another meaning you should keep in mind, which is <kanji>sufficient</kanji>. If you think about it, one foot should be sufficient enough to walk around with if you hop.
data.meaning_hint: Same kanjis and radicals make Jack a happy boy. You should also learn the other meaning as well, though, if you can.
data.reading_mnemonic: What do you wear on your <kanji>foot</kanji>? You wear a <reading>sock</reading> (そく).
data.reading_hint: Just think about your bare feet. Think about them too much. Feel how cold it is on your feet. Now, put on a sock to make it feel better. Ahhh, so nice!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>car</radical> and the kanji <kanji>car</kanji> are exactly the same.
data.meaning_hint: Make sure you study your radicals... though don't do it while driving.
data.reading_mnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading しゃ, we use "shaman".

There's a <kanji>car</kanji>. It's the nicest car you've ever seen. Exquisite, beautiful. Wouldn't you be surprised to learn it belongs to a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ)? Well it does. This shaman charges a premium for his shaman services so he can afford fancy cars like this.
data.reading_hint: The more fantastic you make this car, the better. Also, feel the surprise of a shaman getting out of this car. What a cool dude that shaman is.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>not</radical> and the kanji <kanji>not</kanji> are the same thing! That makes it easy... Not!
data.meaning_hint: Actually, it does make it easy — the radicals make kanji wayyy easier, no?
data.reading_mnemonic:
To remember ふ we'll use the word "fool."

Think of those jokes, where someone says something, then ends with a loud "<kanji>NOTTTT</kanji>!" Someone is doing that to you, over and over. What is that person? That person is a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ).
data.reading_hint: Be sure to feel irritated at that person. Think in your head as they're telling these dumb "not" jokes how much of a fool they are... and how much of a fool you are for sitting here and listening to them.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> <radical>samurai</radical> didn't become leader by sitting around not taking action. He got to where he is by <kanji>doing</kanji>.

data.reading_mnemonic: He was <kanji>doing</kanji> things with <reading>shee</reading>p (し). He is the leader of the sheep clan, and he was able to do more with sheep than anyone else. That's why he's the leader.
data.reading_hint: Picture the leader samurai doing lots of sheep things. He's shearing sheep. He's feeding sheep. He's doing everything you could possibly do, because he's the sheep samurai.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>hole</radical> radical and the <kanji>hole</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Spectacular!

data.reading_hint: Imagine yourself laughing at Ana while you point at her, continuing to jeer as Ana struggles to escape the hole. You’d help Ana out of the hole, but you’re too busy laughing at her.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Under his <radical>roof</radical>, the <radical>king</radical> stares into a <radical>drop</radical> of water containing his greatest <kanji>treasure</kanji>. data.meaning_hint: Picture the treasure reflected in the drop of water. It's marvelous!
data.reading_mnemonic: All of this <kanji>treasure</kanji> is kept in the king's <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). He can't let other people see how much treasure he has, or they'll get mad. Or worse, ask him to share some with his people. Yuck. The treasure stays at home.
data.reading_hint: Picture his home from the outside. It looks like a normal, small castle. But then you walk in and it's a treasure trove of... well, treasure!

data.meaning_mnemonic: <vocabulary>Fourth day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day four</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this combination of kanji, the meaning of the word makes a lot of sense!
data.reading_mnemonic:

data.meaning_mnemonic:
A <kanji>stop</kanji> that's just a <kanji>rest</kanji> (aka not permanent) isn't a straight up stop. You're planning on continuing again. That's why this is a <vocabulary>pause</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>suspension</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>hiatus</vocabulary>.

This is a fairly formal word, and it's often used when an activity or a service is temporarily suspended. For example, you could use it to talk about a class being suspended, a machine suspending its operation, or a pop band going on a hiatus. Check out our common word combinations below for more example usages.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Everything that happened to you <kanji>previous</kanji>ly is just the <vocabulary>tip</vocabulary> of the iceberg, and the important stuff is <vocabulary>ahead</vocabulary> of you, in your <vocabulary>future</vocabulary>. So make sure you put that stuff <vocabulary>first</vocabulary>!

The original meaning of this word is the tip of something, like a pen or a finger. From that meaning, it's expanded into all sorts of meanings, like ahead, first, and future, depending on the context and where it is in the sentence. It can also mean "previous" in formal contexts (and we'll accept that meaning too, but we're not teaching it here because it's more advanced).
data.reading_mnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with the kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

Just <vocabulary>ahead</vocabulary> of you are the <vocabulary>tip</vocabulary>s of a <reading>saw</reading> and a <reading>key</reading>(さき). You have no idea what they're doing there, but both saws and keys have very sharp tips, so watch out you don't hurt yourself. Imagine yourself edging carefully past those saw and key tips.

You can differentiate this from 先ず (まず) because it doesn't have any hiragana on the end.

data.reading_mnemonic:
This word has two possible readings, each with a different nuance, so here are some mnemonics to remember them by:

そうそう is for when you do something quickly or immediately after something else, like 早々にホテルを出る (to leave a hotel quickly) or オープン早々 (as soon as a shop opens). You can remember this because <vocabulary>as soon as</vocabulary> you get to work, you do everything really <vocabulary>quickly</vocabulary>, so your work performance is just <reading>so-so</reading> (そうそう).

はやばや is for when you do something earlier than usual, like going to bed or closing up shop before the usual time. It often appears as 早々と. You can remember this because when you want to close up shop <vocabulary>early</vocabulary>, you say "<reading>Hiya… bye-ya</reading>!" (はやばや) to all the customers who come in. Saying both hi and bye when they walk in is the best way to hint that you're trying close early. Hiya, bye-ya! Yeah, they get the hint.

Whether you're reading an entire book in a single sitting or chugging a whole beer in a single gulp, this thing you're doing is happening all <vocabulary>at once</vocabulary> — in one fell swoop.

一気に refers to one continuous push with no breaks. The image is momentum: doing something in a single burst, rather than something happening instantaneously.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji without okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it usually has the same meaning as the kanji it's spawned from. If you know the kanji meaning you'll also know the vocab meaning. It's <vocabulary>one hundred</vocabulary>!
data.reading_mnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji with no okurigana, it usually uses the kun'yomi reading. Numbers are an exception to this. Numbers use the on'yomi reading, which you've already learned by learning the kanji's reading! How nice!

data.meaning_mnemonic: <kanji>Two</kanji> times <kanji>ten</kanji> <kanji>days</kanji> is <vocabulary>twenty days</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic:
Words that count days use an exceptional reading. The readings for 二十 are exceptions, and 日 takes the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help with that:

If you make one hat for <vocabulary>twenty days</vocabulary> you'll have enough <reading>hats</reading> (はつ) to fill up your <reading>car</reading> (か) completely! That's a lot of days and a lot of hats.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
If you go to (ごと) a <kanji>circle</kanji>, you need to go to it <vocabulary>in its entirety</vocabulary>. That means you have to circle around the <vocabulary>whole</vocabulary> thing. Otherwise, it isn't even a circle. It's like a line or a squiggle or something.

You often hear this word when talking about food – like when biting into a whole apple or accidentally swallowing a grape without chewing. But it's not just for food: you can also use it to talk about memorizing a whole book from beginning to end, for example.
data.reading_mnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading, so you should be able to read this too!

context_sentences.en: Please give me the whole thing, not just half.
context_sentences.ja: 半分じゃなくて丸ごと一つ下さい。

context_sentences.en: I ate a whole pint ice cream all by myself.

context_sentences.en: I got a whole case of mangoes.
context_sentences.ja: マンゴーをケース丸ごともらった。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You <kanji>exit</kanji> but you do it with かける added to it. Just think of that かける as you are exiting while being "cocky" about it. You do this when you want <vocabulary>to go out</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to leave</vocabulary>. You go out in a cocky way!

This word can only be used for a person going out. You can't say a train 出かける'd.
data.reading_mnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading when you learned 出る, so if you can remember that, you should be able to remember this one too!

context_sentences.en: Should we go out somewhere on May 3rd?
context_sentences.ja: 五月三日はどこかに出かける？

context_sentences.en: I go to the mountains on my days off.
context_sentences.ja: 休みの日は山へ出かける。

context_sentences.en: Tanaka-san left for their honeymoon in Hawaii.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If there is a <kanji>big</kanji> <kanji>energy</kanji> it's going to fill up the <vocabulary>atmosphere</vocabulary>! Think about big energies you feel in the atmosphere: thunderstorms, tornadoes, the aurora borealis. Just remember, this is the atmosphere in the sky, not the atmosphere in a fancy restaurant.
data.reading_mnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 大 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Imagine an <vocabulary>atmosphere</vocabulary> consisting entirely of <reading>tie</reading>s and <reading>key</reading>s (たいき), floating around. The ties are getting tangled together, and the keys keep bashing you on the head. Bring back the normal atmosphere, this one's horrible.

context_sentences.en: I developed an allergy because of the polluted atmosphere.

context_sentences.en: There is almost no atmosphere on the moon.

context_sentences.en: The rocket finally left the atmosphere.
context_sentences.ja: ついにロケットが大気から出た。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Doesn't this look like someone on their knees, holding their clasped hands out and begging for something (forgiveness, their life, a promotion, etc.)? That's why this radical is known as the <radical>beggar</radical>.
Can you see the <radical>horns</radical>? You could put these horns on anything. Donkeys, people, monkeys, and so on. That's exactly what we'll be doing when we learn kanji that have horns too.
On the ground there are a bunch of <radical>spikes</radical> coming out. You wouldn't want to step on those now, would you?

You've already learned that 口 means "mouth" and here it's followed by the particle に, which pinpoints a location. The verb する means "to do" so you're doing something to your mouth! Logically enough, this expression is used to describe lots of actions that you do with your mouth. It's often used to mean <vocabulary>to mention</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to say</vocabulary>, but with an added descriptive nuance that you've said something accidentally or unexpectedly, maybe something you shouldn't have said! 🙊

It can also mean other things done with the mouth, like <vocabulary>to eat</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>to drink</vocabulary>!
data.reading_mnemonic: In this expression, 口 is a standalone vocabulary word, so it uses the same reading that you learned when you learned the vocabulary.

context_sentences.en: The queen often says, "you're right."
context_sentences.ja: 女王は、よく「そうね」と口にする。

context_sentences.en: Don't say that name.
context_sentences.ja: その名は口にするな。

context_sentences.en: I got drunk off a little whisky.
context_sentences.ja: ウィスキーを口にしたら、よっぱらってしまった。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You already encountered アメリカ in the previous level, so you should know that it means "America." Put <kanji>north</kanji> in front of this and you get <vocabulary>North America</vocabulary>, just like in English!

Note that 北アメリカ refers to the continent of North America, as opposed to the northern part of the USA. アメリカ on its own usually means the USA, but when it has "north" or "south" in front of it like this, it's always referring to the continents.
data.reading_mnemonic: You might be tempted to read this using the on'yomi readings, but this actually uses kun'yomi. You learned that reading with the kanji, so you should have no problems reading this!

context_sentences.en: Is this river in North America?

context_sentences.en: I went camping in the mountains of North America.
context_sentences.ja: 北アメリカの山でキャンプをしました。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
テレビ comes from the English word <vocabulary>television</vocabulary>. It’s been shortened, which is really common with loanwords in Japanese. The "vi" sound becomes ビ, too.

テレビ is sometimes written as "TV", but even then it’s usually pronounced テレビ.

context_sentences.en: I'm going to speak on TV now.
context_sentences.ja: 今から、テレビでしゃべるんだ。

context_sentences.en: What, you're going on TV?
context_sentences.ja: えっ、テレビに出るの？

context_sentences.en: I'll turn on the TV immediately!
context_sentences.ja: すぐにテレビをつけるよ！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Wanna play <reading>Un</reading>o (うん)? <vocabulary>Yep</vocabulary>! 'Course I do. I always wanna play Uno.

うん is a casual way to say <vocabulary>yes</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>yeah</vocabulary>, so you’d generally use it with friends and family, and in other informal situations.

context_sentences.en: Yep, let's do that!
context_sentences.ja: うん、そうしよう！

context_sentences.en: Yeah, sorry, that's wrong.
context_sentences.ja: うん、ごめん、ちがう。

context_sentences.en: Yep, it's yummy!
context_sentences.ja: うん、おいしい！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Want me to write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい)? <vocabulary>Yes</vocabulary>, of course!

はい is a formal word for "yes". You can use it to let someone know they’re right, or simply that you’re listening, as well as to respond if someone says your name.

context_sentences.en: "Mr. Chandler." "Yes!"
context_sentences.ja: 「チャンドラーさん」「はい！」

context_sentences.en: Yes, thank you very much.
context_sentences.ja: はい、ありがとうございます。

context_sentences.en: Yes, understood.
context_sentences.ja: はい、分かりました。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
ノート is a shortened form of <vocabulary>notebook</vocabulary>, with an extra "o" on the end since you can’t end a word with "t" in Japanese.

You can also use this to mean <vocabulary>notes</vocabulary>, in the context of taking down notes in a class.

context_sentences.en: If it's that notebook you mean, it's on my desk.
context_sentences.ja: そのノートなら、デスクの上だよ。

context_sentences.en: Did you take good notes on the lesson?
context_sentences.ja: レッスン、ちゃんとノートにまとめた？

context_sentences.en: I need an A4 size notebook, you know.
context_sentences.ja: A4サイズのノートがいるんだよね。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
I <reading>saw your Nara</reading> (さようなら), but I then had to say <vocabulary>goodbye</vocabulary>. It was just a short trip, but at least I saw your Nara.

さようなら is a formal way to say goodbye, so a good word to use with colleagues and teachers.

A quick tip before I say goodbye: さようなら often drops its middle う, becoming さよなら.

context_sentences.en: Goodbye, Sensei.
context_sentences.ja: 先生、さようなら。

context_sentences.en: Goodbye, January. Hello, February.
context_sentences.ja: さようなら、一月。こんにちは、二月。

context_sentences.en: So long, and thanks for all the fish.
context_sentences.ja: さようなら、今までサカナをありがとう。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Con ban, wah!</reading> (こんばんは) cries your friend, just as you say <vocabulary>good evening</vocabulary>. There's a report on the evening news about a con ban (yes, that's a ban on conning people). Wah! Exciting stuff.

You can use こんばんは as a greeting from around 5pm or 6pm, in both formal and informal situations.　Watch out for that は on the end, which is pronounced わ here too.

context_sentences.en: Oh, good evening.
context_sentences.ja: あ、こんばんは。

context_sentences.en: Good evening, everyone.
context_sentences.ja: みなさん、こんばんは。

context_sentences.en: Good evening. Nice work today!
context_sentences.ja: こんばんは〜、おつかれさまです！
